name: Wilson, R. Borden
description: We have here a companion piece to Freddie Hocker. This must not be taken too literally, for we only refer to the wonderful record of his at Emory and Henry, when nobody was looking. "Bording" is from Bartow, and is proud of it. We wonder how proud Bartow is of him. So far as is known, there had been no shadows in his fair life until the Football Team went to Cuba this year. The sudden metamorphosis from the U. S. to Cuba was too much for him, and he returned a "sad Budweiser man." Wilson is a steady, consistent student, who has made his efforts count; one of the most dependable men in college.

name: Blanton, Robert Salter
clubs: L. I. from University of Florida, '06
Chairman of Program Committee, Peabody Literary Society, '11-'12
description: Blanton's hardest job is teaching the "Sub-Normal" class something about English. By way of diversion he "shoots" the "profs." In German I, Dr. Crow often refers questions on English syntax to "Red" for explanation, and "Red" always enlightens the rest of the class. If you want to know something, don't ask Mr. Foster, just inquire of "Red." Blanton is an active man in the debating societies, is an earnest student, and knows pedagogics from the front page to the back. We expect to see him soon in a leading role as an instructor of the youth of the State.

name: Casler, Eugene Terry
description: The esteem in which Gene is held is shown by his having the honor of calling the Senior Academic Class to order for its wonderful legislative proceedings. In the three years he has been with us, Gene has made good in every way but talking. He evidently believes that it is the part of a fool to wag one's tongue.

name: Douglass, R. Clyde
description: Here is the finest example of a completely trained recruit from the wicked Gulf Coast City. A diamond in the rough when put into the hands of Dr. Anderson, after four years of Latin and Greek, he now scintillates with classical learning. He has always justly advocated hazing as a part of the college curriculum. "Piggy" has almost as large a share of our liking as any man in school. He is a "dead game sport" in all matters pertaining to college activities. Having rejected his inspiration to the pulpit, he is expected to be seen in the future beaming from a dignified height upon a room full of pupils.

name: Elliott, Walter Gowen
description: "Yens," sometimes spoken of as "Rich Man Elliott, the Baseball Magnate." Few things can "rile" him up, but a poor crowd at a game is one of those few. He has taken the "whole works" in English and utilizes this training chiefly in writing "dope" on the games. "Yens" is a good student, popular, and a good college man. He has mixed pretty successfully in just about all the activities of his school.

name: Henderson, Charles William
description: Bill was blown into the University three years ago, since which time he has succeeded in amusing himself "fussing" the ladies, with studies as an adjunct. Friendly and agreeable, a good student, he is a man of the type which is wanted in every kind of activity. Bill's sense of the artistic, which he expresses by penciling soft curved lines on paper, is always stirred up by light hair and a pair of blue eyes. His popularity takes in both sexes, and is expected to remain with him when he goes into business in Tampa.

name: Jarrell, R. Lee
clubs: Member Board of Student Governors, from Freshman Class, '09-'10
Business Manager University Dramatic Club, '10-'11 and '11-'12
description: The man with a town behind him. Having had experience with railroading, pharmacy, real estate deals, etc., etc., with politics as a side line, Lee is considered the best business man and "bull artist" on the campus. Lee enjoys immense popularity—no man in school is better liked or more deservedly. He has decided on the law for his future, and we suppose Kissimmee will claim him as her leading legal light.

name: Laffitte, L. Sydnor
clubs: Member of Program Committee, Farr Literary Society, '11
description: "Lapp" is the man destined to make Lloyd famous. Four years ago that city grudgingly loaned Florida this man, then a Freshman, now the next Rhodes Scholar from Florida. "Lapp's" course in the University shows there is something in the transmigration of souls theory. Some old Roman has endowed the University with the spirit of his time, and "Lapp" "shoots" Andy and discourses fluently on how they celebrated the Fourth of July in Rome. "Lapp" is a good student, popular, purposeful, and a man. His future lies ahead via Oxford, Johns Hopkins, and Lloyd, Florida. May he catch up with it.

name: Sumter, Leitner A.B. B.A.
description: Leitner has tried to replace "Big Bake" as Prince of Knockers. He is an authority on the uselessness of Latin. In all other subjects he scores high. He tries to make believe that he gets along without studying, but in reality he is just as hard a student as any of the rest. Leitner can be counted on to stick to whatever he decides to do, and to come very near doing it just as he thinks right.

name: Morgan, Louie Robert
description: A South Florida product, striving for higher things than an orange grove. Louie came here swathed in a balmy atmosphere of literary intellectuality, but the icy blasts of Jimmy's criticisms have chilled him to the point of coldness toward anything named literature. He has taken a full course of Crow, and is said to be proficient at "polly vooing," as well as "slinging" the Deutsch and Espanol. Morgan is industrious, not afraid to say what he thinks, has aims and sticks to them. When he gets through telling people where he is from, he will make a success of hardware in Arcadia.

name: Stuart, Mcintosh
description: "John Bull" was imported in 1909, for the purpose of creating a landmark in the University of Florida. Since that time he has nobly fulfilled all the duties pertaining to this office. He has sat at the feet of the Despot of the Slide Rule until he can now figure on that instrument, in a glance, the number of ergs in the toe-muscle of a gnat. He has also become proficient in the art of laughing at an American joke, all slams at his race not to the contrary. "John" is well known to the 'varsity football teams put out by the University during the last four years. He is a good student, is obliging, and we regret to say, decreasingly valuable to the Y. M. C. A. You will be sure to hear of him in the future, doing his duty in whatever line he may enter.

name: Rowlett, Caius Augustus
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer Combined Senior Classes
Vice-President Student Body of Engineering College, 1912-'13
Member of Committee on Caps and Gowns
description: This gentleman, with the Latin prefix, is expected to make Palmetto famous. For four winters he has bent over his slide rule, going "back to the woods" every summer. He has withstood the vicissitudes of mess-hall very successfully and will be returned in as good condition as when he left. This year Caius Augustus is going to show up Cicero by starring in the Senior "Oratoricals." Rowlett is a worker; something of an athlete; well disposed toward his fellow man.

name: Shands, Alvin Glen
description: Allie didn't come to school, he was already here. In the fall of 1907 we caught our first glimpse of him on the football field, where he has since been in evidence every time the good football weather comes around. In his Senior year his grin shares honors with a big "F" in his personal decoration. Allie is another man who seems to have had no trouble in "shooting" his profs. He takes his ability good naturedly, and works his grin twenty-five hours out of the day.

name: Swartz, Howard Villeroys
clubs: Member Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, 1912
description: A fine fellow. Swartz migrated from Lafayette College in February, 1912. At Lafayette College he was captain of the track team, and he has tried to educate us in his line. He seems to have no trouble at all in "shooting up" the "profs." He has done much to make us believe that Agriculture is in the same category with other professions, and has furthered the Y. M. C. A.'s return to nature. This year he ventured on the stage and made strenuous efforts at showing up Nat Goodwin. Swartz has quietly made many friends around the University. A fine fellow.

name: Taylor, Earle Abbott
description: In his college repast, Dummy has been furnished with entrees and desserts by Dr. C. L. Crow. At the present writing, Dummy seems likely to stretch his four years of eligibility to the 'Varsity teams once more, due to following the Manana policy a bit too much. He has mixed in almost every line of college life. In athletics, he has borne his hero-worship as modestly as could be expected of anyone. The host of "fans" who have admired his wonderful work on the gridiron will regret to see him out of the line-up of the "Gators."

name: Thrasher, Leon Brooks
clubs: First Lieutenant and Adjutant of Battalion, 1911-'12
description: Not until the time of "Skiet" did that busy, breezy city of Micanopy have a representative who could sing its adequate praises. "Skiet" won a baseball "F" in 1910 and has never since been seen without it. A touching characteristic of "Skiet" is his generosity about lending his bicycle. Thrasher has strong qualities of patience, persistence and optimism. Though we never hear him boasting of it, he has an enviable record in the Engineering College.

name: Albert, Vidal
description: "Skinny," the man with the motorcycle and the happy smile. Vidal is a worker and a confirmed optimist.

name: White, R. Ray
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer Farr Literary Society, 1912-'13
Member Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, 1912-'13
description: R. R. is a product of a county that produces strawberries. Since his first year here he has persistently, if not consistently, tried to "cop" an oratorical medal, evidently thinking he has the makings of another Webster. White is a "perfect lady," and affects the regular "Rah! Rah!" style. He never says anything strong, and always uses the more moderate "I believe so," instead of "Yes." White is industrious, determined, modest. We expect to have him here next year in Law School.

name: Brooks, Arthur Cecil
description: He is a native Floridian, born in Bradford County, where he spent the greater portion of his earlier days. His preliminary education was received in the public schools of Bradford. Later he attended Columbia College. Being inclined toward the field of education, he pursued his studies in the South Florida Normal Institute at Dade City, and in various teachers' institutes. He has taught in the public schools of several counties, and even unto this day, he has maintained the dignity of a seasoned pedagogue. As a hobby in law he is especially fitted in the subject of Bills and Notes. While he is unusually studious, he nevertheless finds time to occasionally favor the ladies with the charm and grace of his presence.

name: Cox, Boyd Abel
description: Behold the offering that Washington County sent to the college for men! This is Cox, the ladies' man in college. But then we should not speak of the fair-eyed maid, or of the writing at the train. Cox undoubtedly has won more friends in his two years than any fellow in college, and, further, he holds his friends. Surely old Washington sent us her best in Cox. He had the distinction of uniting in the holy bonds of matrimony couples of different nationalities while serving in his capacity as Notary Public. Like the turtle, he is sure to win in the race of life.

name: Clayton, Orlando J.
description: Orlando is a long, lean, lanky, and yet, quite a handsome Hubby. Before coming to the University he sacrificed a good portion of his time in the teachers' profession, serving to elevate the youth of Jefferson county. He was also a faithful employee in the service of Uncle Sam. His stability and untiring plodding as a student have placed to his credit the distinction and honor of making the highest marks of any student in the College of Law. Any person seeking any form of redress in the courts of justice may justly repose his faith and confidence in Orlando.

name: Devane, Enoch Clayborn
description: "Andy," or "Tiny," as he is popularly known, left the classic halls of Washington and Lee to seek the sunny campus of Florida. "Tiny" is fond of reading case law and the Plant City Courier. He is our leading prohibitionist, and is never so happy as when working for the great cause. "Tiny" claims to have built all the rock roads in Hillsborough County, but on account of his frail and delicate physique, he long since gave up such strenuous employment. He is always willing to wager that he can alienate the affections of the other fellow's Dulcinea—but. His chivalrous conduct, gentlemanly demeanor, and legal learning will no doubt make him one of South Florida's favorite sons.

name: Diamond, Charles Peter
description: The above is a cut of one of Santa Rosa's most illustrious youths, familiarly known around the University City as "C. P." Like many of our legal lights of note, "C. P.," too, is an outgrowth of the teachers' profession. In appearance and animation he is next akin to Adonis; in attitude he has a striking resemblance to an owl, silence being his chief characteristic. He is a strong advocate of the independence of the judiciary, Jeffersonian democracy, and world peace, as well as divers other subjects which engage his thinking capacity in idle moments. Of him we expect a foremost citizen and lawyer.

name: Garrett, George Palmer
clubs: Vice-President, John Marshall Debating Society, first semester 1911-12
President, John Marshall Debating Society, second semester
Critic, John Marshall Debating Society, second semester 1912-13
description: If there is a Cosmopolite in the university it is "Ponch." He has haunted the American continent from the seaport towns of the northeast to the mines and citrus groves of the west. Finally, he reconciled himself to the environments of Osceola County, Florida, where he enjoyed a remunerative Abstract business before coming to the university. To him we are indebted for establishing on a firm basis our college paper, The Florida Alligator. He protests with great vim and vigor against any "insinuations" in class meetings. "Ponch" has an analytical mind, is strong in the intricacies of the law, and bids fair to become one of Florida's eminent dignitaries.

name: Gibson, James Bond Jr.
high school: High School of the City of Mulberry
motto: "the half has never yet been told."
description: When upon this specimen of humanity you gaze, you gaze upon America's Citizen King. His natural ability, his masterful physique, his magnetic personality, and his charm of manner, coupled with the fact that he is a graduate of the exalted High School of the City of Mulberry, admirably fit him for all the offices, private and public, of peace and war. "Gib," unlike Demosthenes, is a natural born orator (?). In the John Marshall, he is a Webster; in class, "the half has never yet been told." He is popular in Epworth League circles as well as on the campus. To the bar of Tampa all the parts and properties of this wonderful human mechanism are hereby bequeathed, devised, and willed in fee. In behalf of generations yet unborn, and for the sake of the common welfare of all mankind, we plead with you to preserve and keep our Lord and King.

name: Godwin, John Luther
description: He entered Florida after a brief sojourn at the University of Georgia. While he is strong in all branches of the law, his accurate knowledge of domestic relations will probably lead him to specialize in Divorce Law. He also demonstrated considerable histrionic ability in playing the part of Macbeth on that memorable night at Newnan's Lake. His chief ambition, however, is to discover the most striking combination of white and black that the tailor can produce.

name: Hocker, Frederick Roche
description: Fred hailed to us from Washington and Lee, whose historic halls he long since learned to revere. He is both democratic and aristocratic in his make-up, and has hosts of friends in all parts of Florida. Since his election as student cheer leader, Florida has won more victories on the gridiron and diamond than in any other year of her history. As an angler, his ability is superior; as a ball-room devotee his grace is beyond degree. Gainesville's fairer ones are unanimous in the decision that "Freddie" is handsome—on this point, however, there is a little conflict of authority. Unlike most animals, he perspires on top of his head only. His chief animation is his smile. In Fred we can see one who is to be among our foremost jurist-citizens.

name: Householder, Earnest F.
description: The events of his illustrious life in the University City would make a fair sized book on Romance and Adventure. A thousand times has the campus rung with the music of his "shippa-hoy;" a thousand times has he presented his saucy, banty-rooster-like figure in front of Miller's and in the parlors of the social caste for "de luxe" discussion and entertainment. In Plant City it was otherwise (there he was a defunct sport). His histrionic ability is recognized throughout the State, especially in Tallahassee, where our sisters were unanimous in designating him "Cutey." As a lawyer, Florida is blessed, and is to be congratulated upon having such a mass of human perfection on her soil.

name: Kennedy, Walter M.
clubs: Vice-President Combined Senior Classes and Student Body
description: Kindly let your eyes wander for an instant to the flaming portrait herewith portrayed. You are right, this is "Red," or, more properly speaking, "Red" Kennedy of Umatilla. He is an all-round scout and a firm believer in lessons not found in books. Added to his talent of story telling, which by the way, is unsurpassed, "Red" is probably the best financier in college. Not only does he show rare ability of this quality in school, but his ability to make both ends meet out of school speaks for itself.

name: Lawler, Samuel Watson
description: Commonly called "Wat," a product of Southern College, graduating from that fountain of knowledge in the year of our Lord, 1910; a disciple of George Ade, and our leading exponent of peg-leg trousers. He is an all-round athlete and sport. "Wat" is deeply appreciative, however, of the logic and reason in law, and is fast attuning his mind to the lofty harmonies of his profession.

name: Langston, Bernard Gainer
clubs: Winner Children of the Confederacy Medal
description: He is our oldest inhabitant, having entered the University before the paint was dry, and has remained ever since. As they are rapidly adding new colleges to the University, we will probably have him for years to come. It would reflect on the people of the fair little city of Chipley if they should fail to celebrate his advent there as a lawyer. While nature evidently planned him for a supreme judge, he will likely be more noted for his 'winning ways.'

name: Lischkoff, Leon N.
description: "Lisch" comes to us from Tulane University. While in New Orleans he laid the scene of many of his didactic narrations which he never forgets when assembled for a few idle moments with his fellow Bull Artists. Like Oliver Wendell Holmes, "Lisch" revels in conversation. Has never been caught loafing around Miller's. His life work will center around the securing of a post office for his home town, for which purpose he is now circulating a petition. Later he expects to personally present it to Postmaster-General Burleson, and has no doubts but what it will be granted.

name: Earnest F. Magaha
description: The law is nothing new to him. For several years prior to entering the University he was attired in the dignified robe of a Justice of the Peace. His grim and serious countenance enables him to 'gaze into the eye-balls of the fierce Numidian lion, even as a boy upon a laughing girl.' We know no more consistent student than him. The only difference we can figure out between him and the great W. J. Bryan, is that he believes in the independence of the judiciary. In all probability his lot will be cast in Santa Rosa, whose hills and valleys he has long since learned to love.

name: McCaskill, James Marshall
clubs: President local and State Collegiate Prohibition Oratorical Leagues
Member John Marshall Debating Teams, 1912 and 1913
Second place winner State oratorical contest, Lake City, 1912
description: He hailed from Louisiana. Before taking up the study of the law he was in the service of various railway companies in various capacities. He is our orator whose silvery tongue can win the turtle dove from its mate, or the angle worm from its burrow. His tonsorial shortcomings rival that of our Dean, and his dignified carriage makes the stately stride of Doctor Murphree look like the gambols of a month-old puppy. To be a great lawyer is his one dominating passion.

name: Clabourne, M'c. Phipps
major: Bachelor of Laws, B.A., University of Mississippi
description: For two years "McC" endeavored to impress us that he lived in Tampa. At last, however, his dark secret was discovered, and we found that he, who claimed to be an habitual city boulevarder of Ybor City, was, in truth, a follower of the furrows of Terra Ceia. "McC" was the chief instrumentality through which President Wilson gained his strength in Florida. He has been a progressive student, and the local bar of Tampa will find in him none the less a progressive, worthy, and brilliant attorney.

name: Franklin, Riherd
clubs: Reporter and Sergeant-at-Arms for the John Marshall Debating Society
description: "Ri" is a son of old Kentucky, a former student of Georgetown (Ky.) College, and Stetson University. He has taught school in different parts of the country, closing his days as an active pedagogue in Suwannee, Pasco, DeSoto, Wakulla, and Lafayette counties, Florida. "Ri" is of a mild and gentle disposition, and hates a fight like a "nigger" hates 'possum. His friends are many and, to his memory, dear—to his heart indelible. He is an angler of repute, and his chief habitat is the banks of Hog Town Creek. In "Ri" we have found a big-hearted fellow, a man of a broad sense of right and justice, a companion and friend.

name: Bascom, V. Mathis
description: Bascom V. is a typical Florida cracker, an easy-going, cheerful, and handsome lad. Coming, as he does, from the environments of a well-ordered country life, he has found himself happily fitted for the subject of "Real Property," in which he will very likely specialize. He is not what is termed a "Lady's Man," but judging from the experiences he relates, he is evidently versed in the winsome arts. His chief delight on the campus has been to assemble with the crowd on the Thomas Hall corner, where he can exercise his wit in levity. A good bit of his past experience has been as a pedagogue, but he is now a loyal admirer of the ancient science of the law.

name: Miller, Samuel Oscar
motto: "Speak of me as I am; nothing extenuate."
major: Bachelor of Laws, A.B., Bachelor of Arts
clubs: President of the combined Senior Classes at the University of Florida
